subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The conversion section is the chapter's most sustained procedural strand: children convert hours to minutes and minutes to seconds using double number lines, then solve eight mixed conversion problems (e.g. 2 hours 45 minutes to minutes, 320 seconds to minutes and seconds). This is directly unit conversion within the time measurement system, with the number line providing the intermediate concrete scaffold before symbolic computation.

The seconds-as-a-unit section asks children to estimate whether everyday activities (blinking, counting 1–20, melting ice) take seconds or minutes, then physically verify predictions by timing themselves skipping rope in 10 seconds, writing a word, and running 100 m. The estimate-then-verify structure — using the body and a clock as measurement tools — is precisely the competency of devising and testing estimation strategies for time.

adjunct_competencies.justification: The conversion work is built on a multiplicative relationship (1 hour = 60 minutes, 1 minute = 60 seconds), and the double number line makes this structure visible — scaling up by multiplying and scaling down by dividing. Children apply multiplication and division of whole numbers to convert between time units, using arithmetic operations as the engine of the conversion rather than as an end in itself.

The elapsed-time word problems (finding when Jyoti returns home, when Ragini started homework, when a picnic group returns, and so on) are daily-life problems requiring addition or subtraction of mixed-unit time durations. These problems are the chapter's primary context for applying time knowledge to realistic situations with a numerical answer.

The teacher note for the word-problem section explicitly directs children to solve orally rather than by column algorithms, and the worked example (08:00 to 09:05 hours solved by decomposing into 1 whole hour plus 5 minutes) models mental computation as the preferred method. Children are implicitly selecting mental strategies over written procedures throughout this section.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to measure and reason about time by converting between seconds, minutes, and hours, estimating durations, and solving elapsed-time problems — the operations that define CG-3's measurement strand for the time attribute.
